items.q: What is the difference between isometric and perspective drawing?
items.correct: Isometric keeps receding lines parallel at 30 degrees with no convergence; perspective makes receding lines converge to vanishing points so distant parts look smaller.
items.misconception: Isometric drawings have vanishing points just like perspective drawings.
items.random_wrong: Isometric drawing can only be done freehand without instruments.
items.why: Both methods show three faces of an object, so students merge them; the presence or absence of vanishing points is the defining difference.

items.q: In orthographic projection, what is the plan view?
items.correct: The view of the object seen from directly above, drawn in projection with the front and side views.
items.misconception: The plan view is the front face of the product because it is planned first.
items.random_wrong: The plan view is the project time plan attached to the drawing.
items.why: The word 'plan' misleads students; anchoring it as the bird's-eye view from above prevents mislabelling working drawings.

items.q: Does a 1:2 scale drawing show the object larger or smaller than real life?
items.correct: Smaller — 1:2 means one unit on the drawing represents two units in reality, so the drawing is half size; 2:1 would be twice full size.
items.misconception: A 1:2 drawing is twice as big as the real object.
items.random_wrong: 1:2 means two separate copies of the drawing are required.
items.why: Students read the ratio backwards; remembering drawing:real as the order of the numbers resolves every scale question.

items.q: In isometric projection, receding lines are drawn at what angle to the horizontal?
items.why: Correct answer: 30 degrees. “45 degrees” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does an orthographic drawing show?
items.correct: Related 2D views such as front, side and plan
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Related 2D views such as front, side and plan. “A single 3D pictorial view” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Two-point perspective uses how many vanishing points?
items.why: It's Two. “One” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the purpose of rendering a sketch?
items.correct: To show form and material using tone and texture
items.random_wrong: To calculate the cost of materials
items.why: Correct answer: To show form and material using tone and texture. “To add dimensions for manufacture” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A drawing at a scale of 1:5 shows a part 40 mm long. How long is the real part?
items.why: Exact answer: 200 mm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
